title: api: build local AO3 API proxy for agent loop
description:
## Context
The agent needs a clean interface to AO3 that decouples the agent loop from
AO3's lack of a real API and its TOS prohibition on automation. A local API
proxy mirrors AO3's shape so agent code is clean and testable.

## Task
1. Design the API surface:
   - Browse: GET /fandoms/{name}/works, GET /tags/{tag}/works, GET /works/{id}
   - Metrics: GET /works/{id}/stats (kudos, hits, bookmarks, subscriptions)
   - Comments: GET /works/{id}/comments, GET /works/{id}/chapters/{n}/comments  
   - Publish: POST /works (queues for human review, does NOT auto-post)
   - Profile: GET /users/{name}/works, GET /users/{name}/stats
   - Search: GET /search?q=...&fandom=...&tags=...
2. Build as a FastAPI local server
3. For read endpoints: wire through to ao3-api (unofficial Python library)
4. For write endpoints: queue to local filesystem for human review
5. Stub/mock mode for testing without hitting AO3
6. Rate limiting to respect AO3's servers

## Acceptance Criteria
- FastAPI server runs locally with all endpoints defined
- Read endpoints fetch real data from AO3 (with caching + rate limits)
- Write endpoints queue to local files
- Mock mode returns realistic test data
- Agent code only imports from this API, never from ao3-api directly

title: pipeline: adapt autonovel writing pipeline for fanfiction
description:
## Context
The existing autonovel pipeline writes 80k-word novels from scratch. For fanfiction,
we need a lighter pipeline: shorter works (one-shots: 3-15k words), fandom context
instead of worldbuilding, and characterization accuracy for existing characters.

## Task
1. Analyze which autonovel tools are reusable vs need adaptation
2. Create a fanfic drafting pipeline that:
   - Takes fandom context (character profiles, canon facts) as input instead of world.md
   - Supports one-shots (single file) and multi-chapter works
   - Uses identity/self.md and voice_priors for voice consistency
   - Loads few-shot bank passages as style examples
3. Adapt evaluate.py for fanfic norms (characterization accuracy, fandom voice)
4. Keep the full anti-slop pipeline (ANTI-SLOP.md, ANTI-PATTERNS.md)
5. Simplify the revision loop for shorter works (fewer cycles needed)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Can draft a one-shot fanfic from: fandom + prompt + identity context
- Evaluation scores characterization accuracy (are characters in-character?)
- Anti-slop detection passes on output
- Revision pipeline works for shorter works

title: publish: build AO3 post preparation pipeline
description:
## Context
After writing, the agent needs to prepare a complete "ready to post" package that
the human can copy-paste into AO3. This includes AO3-specific formatting, tag
selection, summary writing, and author's notes in the pen name's voice.

## Task
1. Build AO3 post formatter (markdown → AO3 HTML)
2. Build tag generator (fandom tags, character tags, relationship tags, freeform tags)
   based on content analysis
3. Build summary generator (hook-style, under 500 chars, in the author's voice)
4. Build author's notes generator (in pen name's voice — casual, personal, not AI-like)
5. Package everything into a single "post package" the human can review and post
6. Track publication history (what was posted, when, which work, chapter)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Given a finished fic, outputs a complete post package (title, tags, summary,
  author's notes, formatted body)
- Author's notes read as human-written (this is a critical anti-detection surface)
- Publication history is tracked in a local database/file

title: human: create AO3 account and provide credentials
description:
## Context
AO3 prohibits automated posting but allows manual posting. We need a human to create
an AO3 account with the pen name, and provide credentials for the metrics scraping API.

## Task (Human)
1. Choose a pen name (agent can propose options, human picks)
2. Create AO3 account with that pen name
3. Write a brief bio for the profile
4. Provide AO3 username/password for metrics scraping (store in .env)
5. Set up the AO3 profile page

## Acceptance Criteria
- AO3 account exists and is active
- Credentials are in .env (AO3_USERNAME, AO3_PASSWORD)
- Profile has a bio that fits the persona

title: feedback: build AO3 metrics scraper and comment parser
description:
## Context
The learning loop needs real feedback data from AO3. We need to scrape metrics
(kudos, comments, bookmarks, hits, subscriptions) and parse comments for
qualitative signal.

## Task
1. Build AO3 metrics scraper using ao3-api or similar unofficial library
   - Fetch work-level metrics: kudos, comments, bookmarks, hits, subscriptions
   - Fetch per-chapter metrics where available
   - Track metrics over time (daily snapshots)
2. Build comment parser
   - Extract comment text and metadata (date, user, chapter)
   - Sentiment analysis (positive, constructive, negative)
   - Identify quoted passages (what lines readers highlight)
   - Identify specific feedback (characterization, pacing, voice, plot)
3. Store feedback data in structured format (JSON/JSONL)
4. Build feedback summary generator (per-work and per-chapter digests)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Can fetch metrics for a given AO3 work URL
- Can parse comments and extract structured feedback
- Stores time-series data for trend analysis
- Produces actionable feedback summaries

title: skills: tailor copied skills for Python/autonovel project
description:
## Context
Skills were copied from lb-agent-factory (TypeScript/Next.js project). This is
a Python/uv project. Several skills reference wrong tooling, paths, and conventions.

## Task
1. lint/SKILL.md: Replace biome references with ruff; update Python conventions
2. orient/SKILL.md: Rewrite for this project's structure (autonovel + agentic author)
3. impl/SKILL.md: Remove TypeScript-specific references, adapt for Python
4. test/SKILL.md: Adapt for pytest conventions
5. branch/SKILL.md: Simplify if needed for this project's branching model
6. Review hooks/*.sh for hardcoded paths or lb-agent-factory references
7. Update .claude/settings.json if hook paths need adjustment

## Acceptance Criteria
- All skills reference Python/ruff/pytest/uv, not TypeScript/biome/bun
- orient skill describes this project accurately
- Hooks work in this repo (no broken paths)
- No references to lb-agent-factory remain

title: learning: build prompt evolution and few-shot bank system
description:
## Context
This is the core differentiator — the part that makes the agent actually learn
rather than just retry. The learning engine takes feedback data and updates the
agent's writing system: prompt evolution, few-shot bank curation, evaluation
weight adjustment, and self-reflection.

## Task
1. Prompt evolution system:
   - Maintain a bank of system prompt variants
   - After each feedback cycle, generate mutations of the best-performing prompts
   - Score new prompt variants by writing test passages and evaluating them
   - Keep/discard based on evaluation (+ reader feedback when available)
2. Few-shot bank:
   - Store the best passages tagged by quality dimension (dialogue, tension, etc.)
   - Passages that got quoted in reader comments are highest-value
   - Retrieval by relevance to current writing task
   - Prune passages that led to AI-detectable patterns
3. Eval weight adjustment:
   - Track which eval dimensions correlate with reader engagement
   - Shift weights toward what readers actually respond to
   - E.g., if readers love dialogue but eval over-weights prose density, adjust
4. Self-reflection:
   - After each feedback cycle, agent updates identity/self.md
   - "What I learned from this publication" → changes to approach

## Acceptance Criteria
- Prompt evolution can generate, test, and select prompt variants
- Few-shot bank stores, tags, and retrieves passages
- Eval weights update based on feedback correlation
- Self.md updates after each cycle with specific learnings

title: identity: design agent identity system and voice priors
description:
## Context
The agent needs a persistent creative identity that evolves over time. This is the
"self" that differentiates it from generic Claude output. It includes voice priors,
literary influences, strengths/weaknesses, and a self-model that updates after each
feedback cycle.

## Task
1. Design the self.md schema (voice description, influences, strengths, weaknesses,
   growth areas, reader relationship model)
2. Design voice_priors.json schema (quantified style parameters: sentence length
   distribution, dialogue-to-prose ratio, interiority depth, metaphor density, etc.)
3. Design pen_name.md (persona voice for author's notes, bio, interaction style)
4. Design inspirations.md (literary influences and what the agent takes from each)
5. Build the identity/ directory with initial templates
6. Write the self-reflection update mechanism (how self.md evolves after feedback)

## Acceptance Criteria
- identity/ directory exists with self.md, voice_priors.json, pen_name.md, inspirations.md
- Schemas are documented and make sense for the learning loop
- Self-reflection mechanism is specified (input: feedback data, output: self.md updates)

title: infra: bootstrap repo with skills, hooks, CLAUDE.md, and PLAN.md
loop: design and build the core author loop orchestrator
description:
## Context
The core loop is the heartbeat of the system. It connects all subsystems
(identity, writing, publishing, feedback, learning, strategy) into a single
automated cycle. This is the "outer loop" that makes the agent self-improving.

## Task
1. Design the loop orchestrator (similar to run_pipeline.py but for the full cycle)
2. Define state machine: PLAN → WRITE → REVIEW → PUBLISH → WAIT → COLLECT → LEARN → PLAN
3. Define state.json schema for the loop
4. Build the orchestrator that:
   - Reads current state
   - Executes the appropriate phase
   - Transitions to next state
   - Handles human checkpoints (PUBLISH requires human action)
   - Resumes after human action
5. Wire all subsystems together

## Acceptance Criteria
- Loop can run from PLAN through LEARN and back to PLAN
- Human checkpoint at PUBLISH pauses and resumes cleanly
- State is persisted and recoverable
- Each phase calls the appropriate subsystem

title: strategy: build fandom analysis and story planning system
description:
## Context
The agent needs to make strategic decisions: what fandom to write in, what type of
story, what ship/genre, when to post, whether to start a series. These decisions
should be informed by feedback data, fandom trends, and the agent's identity.

## Task
1. Fandom analysis:
   - Given a fandom, analyze what's popular (ships, tropes, genres)
   - Identify underserved niches (demand without supply)
   - Track trending tags and themes
2. Story planning:
   - Generate story ideas that fit: fandom demand + agent's voice + growth goals
   - Decide between one-shot vs multi-chapter
   - For series: plan arc across multiple works
3. Release strategy:
   - Posting frequency recommendations based on engagement patterns
   - Timing (when are readers most active?)
   - Balance exploitation (write what works) vs exploration (try new things)
4. Growth tracking:
   - Subscriber count over time
   - Which works brought new subscribers
   - Reader retention (do readers come back?)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Can analyze a fandom and recommend story directions
- Generates story ideas aligned with agent identity + reader demand
- Tracks growth metrics and adjusts strategy
